coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of cucurbit[6]uril/p-aminobenzenesulfonamide carbon quantum dots and selective recognition of 2,4-dinitrophenol in water
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Xiaoqian
Yuan, Shangwei
Ye, Yanan
Yang, Xinan
Xiao, Xin
Ma, Peihua
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a cucurbit[n]uril carbon quantum dots (Q[6]-CQDs) has been synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal method by using cucurbit[6]uril (Q[6]) and p-aminobenzenesulfonamide (SN), which characterized by UV–vis, FL, IR, XPS, XRD and TEM. The 1H NMR spectra clearly showed that the rigid macrocyclic skeleton of Q[6] was retained during the synthesis of Q[6]-CQDs. In addition, the Q[6]@SN complex was confirmed by X-ray crystallography. The particle size of the prepared Q[6]-CQDs is about ∼ 3.3 nm, which emits strong blue fluorescence and has good water solubility. Subsequently, 2,4-dinitrophenol (2,4-DNP) in aqueous solution was identified and detected by the change of fluorescence intensity of Q[6]-CQDs with a detection limit of 0.15 μM (27.62 μg/L), lower than the relevant standard of USEPA (300 μg/L), and the calculated recovery rate was between 98.90 % and 106.20 %, which provides a new analytical approach for the monitoring of 2,4-DNP in water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cucurbit[6]uril
2,4-dinitrophenol
Carbon quantum dots
Nano-fluorescent probe
Crystal structure

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Description of the crystal structure of SN@Q[6]
Fluorescence detection of 2,4-DNP by the Q[6]-CQDs
Quantitative detection of 2,4-DNP in polluted water

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of Q[6]-CQDs by hydrothermal method and recognition of 2,4-DNP.

(a) UV-vis absorption spectrum of Q[6]-CQDs. Inset: Photographs of the solution of the Q[6]-CQDs under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right); (b) the emission spectra of the Q[6]-CQDs under different excitation wavelengths; (c) HRTEM image of Q[6]-CQDs: Particle size and morphology analysis (Inset: Particle size distribution); (d) HRTEM imaging of Q[6]-CQDs.
(a) XPS spectra of the Q[6]-CQDs; (b-d) high resolution spectra of C 1s, O 1s and S 2p, respectively.
(a) Representative ortep diagram of complex 1; (b, c) the interaction diagram of Q[6] and SN; (d) the three-dimensional stacking diagram of complex 1 in the c direction.

(a) Fluorescence response of Q[6]-CQDs (40 μg/ mL; λmax = 351 nm) to phenolic compounds (phenolic compounds (aq.), 30 μM.), Inset: Fluorescence images of Q[6]-CQDs without (left) and with 2,4-DNP (right) under 365 nm UV light; (b) Fluorescence spectra of probe-2,4-DNP with coexisting phenolic compounds; (c) UV-vis absorption spectra obtained for Q[6]-CQDs upon increasing the concentration of 2,4-DNP in water; (d) UV–vis absorption spectra of various 2,4-DNP and emission peaks of Q[6]-CQDs.
(a) Titration fluorescence spectra of Q[6]-CQDs ((40 μg/ mL) with 2,4-DNP (0, 1, 3,…20, 25, 30 μM); (b) Fluorescence responses of CQDs at different concentrations to 2,4-DNP.
Comparison of LOD with the reported methods of 2-DNP determination.

row.entry: GC-MS with hydrogen as carrier gas

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection results of 2,4-dinitrophenol in polluted water.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of cucurbit[6]uril/p-aminobenzenesulfonamide carbon quantum dots and selective recognition of 2,4-dinitrophenol in water

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Macrocyclic and Supramolecular Chemistry of Guizhou Province, Guizhou University, Guiyang 550025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Macrocyclic and Supramolecular Chemistry of Guizhou Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aKey Laboratory of Macrocyclic and Supramolecular Chemistry of Guizhou Province, Guizhou University, Guiyang 550025, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a cucurbit[n]uril carbon quantum dots (Q[6]-CQDs) has been synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal method by using cucurbit[6]uril (Q[6]) and p-aminobenzenesulfonamide (SN), which characterized by UV–vis, FL, IR, XPS, XRD and TEM. The 1H NMR spectra clearly showed that the rigid macrocyclic skeleton of Q[6] was retained during the synthesis of Q[6]-CQDs. In addition, the Q[6]@SN complex was confirmed by X-ray crystallography. The particle size of the prepared Q[6]-CQDs is about ∼ 3.3 nm, which emits strong blue fluorescence and has good water solubility. Subsequently, 2,4-dinitrophenol (2,4-DNP) in aqueous solution was identified and detected by the change of fluorescence intensity of Q[6]-CQDs with a detection limit of 0.15 μM (27.62 μg/L), lower than the relevant standard of USEPA (300 μg/L), and the calculated recovery rate was between 98.90 % and 106.20 %, which provides a new analytical approach for the monitoring of 2,4-DNP in water.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [1,2] represent a novel classical photoluminescence nanomaterials with the advantages of excellent optical stability, good water solubility, good biocompatibility and low toxicity [3,4], which CQDs have been widely used in the fields biological imaging, biomedicine, textile industry, sensors, photocatalysis [5–13]. For example, Emam et al. studied the effect of heteroatom changes in the main skeleton of CQDs on the nucleation affinity of CQDs as anti-inflammatory and anticancer drugs [14]. They also prepared a CQDs with stable bright green fluorescence, which can be used as green fluorescent markers in the detection of Zn2+ and Hg2+ [15]. El-Metwaly et al. prepared an effective textile AgNPs-CQDs @ Cotton using silver nanoparticles-doped carbon quantum dots (AgNPs-CQDs), which exhibited excellent UV-resistance and antimicrobial activity [16]. At present, many techniques for the synthesis of CQDs have been reported, such as oxidation, discharge and hydrothermal methods [17,18], in which, hydrothermal method provides a mild, efficient and widely applicable method for the preparation of CQDs [19,20]. The CQDs synthesized by hydrothermal method can generate hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl, ether and other functional groups on the surface, which endow these nanoparticles with excellent reactivity.

Cucurbit[n]urils (Q[n] (n = 5–8, 10, 13–15)) are a class of classical macrocyclic molecules with rigid structures [21–23], which have near-neutral and different sizes of cavities, and negatively charged carbonyl ports, which exhibit a good affinity for organic molecules and metal ions [24,25], and compared with other macrocyclic hosts, Q[n] has good molecular selective binding ability in water. It is more attractive because of its green and non-toxic characteristics and is an environment-friendly material and plays an important role in supramolecular chemistry and materials science [26]. In recent years, cucurbit[n]urils have been proved to be good raw materials for the synthesis of carbon quantum dots [27]. The combination of Q[n]s and CQDs aims to manipulate the optical properties and self-assembly behavior of CQDs, and realize the easy functionalization and good water solubility of CQDs [28].

It is well known that 2,4-dinitrophenol, as one of the nitrophenol compounds, is one of the main environmental organic pollutants widely distributed in the ecological environment and has toxic effects on humans and ecosystems [29,30]. Therefore, it is very vital to develop a sensitive and selective method for the analysis of 2,4-DNP. At present, the methods for detecting 2,4-DNP include Gas chromatography–mass spectrometry [31], electroanalysis [32], electrochemical method [33], spectrophotometry [34], and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [35]. Their detection limits are shown in Table 1 .

In this paper, water-soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots (Q[6]-CQDs) were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method using Q[6] and SN at 180 °C for 10 h (Fig. 1 , for details, see the Experimental section in Supporting Information). Their structures are shown in Fig. S1. Then, Q[6]-CQDs can be served as a fluorescent probe for the detection of environmental pollutant 2,4-DNP. At the same time, the possible interaction mechanism between Q[6]-CQDs and 2,4-DNP was discussed, providing a theoretical basis for the development of new fluorescent probes for the detection of phenolic pollutants. Compared with the previous literature (Table 1), this method has the advantages of simple operation, good selectivity and strong operability, and has a good application prospect.

The Q[6]-CQDs were synthesized from SN and Q[6] using a hydrothermal method. In the UV-vis absorption spectrum, the Q[6]-CQDs exhibited maximum absorption peaks at 260 and 441 nm in an aqueous solution. The absorption peak at 260 nm was attributed to the π–π* transition of CC bonds and the one at 441 nm to the n–π* transition of the N and/or S species [36]. The Q[6]-CQDs solution was light yellow under natural light and exhibits a blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light (365 nm) (Fig. 2 a). Fig. 2 b shows the fluorescence emission spectrum of the Q[6]-CQDs at an excitation wavelength in the range of 235–280 nm. When the excitation wavelength is 260 nm, the Q[6]-CQDs exhibited the highest fluorescence emission intensity at the emission wavelength of 350 nm. UV-vis adsorption and emission behavior were not observed in the aqueous solutions of pristine Q[6].

The functional groups of Q[6]-CQDs were further characterized by infrared spectroscopy (IR) (Fig. S2). The absorption peak at 3569 cm−1 confirmed the presence of OH and NH bonds. The strong absorption peak at 1734 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of the CO bonds, whereas the peaks observed at 1479, 1326, 1236, and 1099 cm−1 indicated the presence of CC, CN, CO, and SO bonds, respectively. The absorption peak at 552 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of the CS bonds. The NH, CC, SO, and CS bonds in the IR spectrum preliminarily indicated the successful synthesis of the Q[6]-CQDs.

The transmission electron microscopy image shows that the synthesized carbon dots were spherical, uniformly dispersed, and non-aggregated, and the average particle size was ∼3.3 nm (Fig. 2 c). Fig. 2 d shows that the surface of Q[6]-CQDs had an ordered lattice structure observed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). The interplanar spacing was ∼ 0.22 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of the hexagonal lattice on the graphite carbon plane [37].

In addition, the X-ray powder diffraction of the Q[6]-CQDs has an obvious diffraction peak at about 23.3°, which attributed to amorphous carbon composed in a considerably random fashion the disorder carbon of Q[6]-CQDs (Fig.S3) [38]. The Quantum yield and decay time of Q[6]-CQDs are shown in the Fig. S4 and S5.

The elemental composition and surface functional groups in the Q[6]-CQDs were determined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 3 a shows that the XPS spectrum of the Q[6]-CQDs exhibits four typical peaks at 287.08, 532.08, 400.08, and 169.08 eV, which were attributed to C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and S 2p, respectively, indicating that the Q[6]-CQDs mainly contains four elements: C, O, N and S. The higher C and O content confirmed that the Q[6]-CQDs were rich in oxygen-containing functional groups. Fig. 3 b shows the fine C 1s spectrum with absorptions at 284.7, 285.6, 287.3, and 288.9 eV corresponding to the CC/CC, CS, CN, and CO bonds, respectively. In the N 1s spectrum (Fig. S6), the absorption peaks observed at 399.9 and 401.6 eV can be attributed to the CN and NH bonds, respectively. In the O 1s spectrum (Fig. 3 c), the absorption peaks at 531.5 and 532.5 eV correspond to the CO and COH bonds, respectively. In addition, the absorption peaks observed in the S 2p spectrum (Fig. 3 d) at 168.3 and 169.3 eV confirmed that sulfur exists in the form of -CSO3– and -CSO2–, respectively [36]. The presence of these hydrophilic functional groups enables Q[6]-CQDs to be highly dispersed and stable in aqueous solution. At the same time, the original proton peak of Q[6] and SN were clearly observed in the 1H NMR spectrum obtained for the Q[6]-CQDs (Fig. S7), which indicates that the rigid macrocyclic skeleton of Q[6] and the structure of SN remained intact during the hydrothermal reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Description of the crystal structure of SN@Q[6]
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 a is the representative ortep diagram of SN@Q[6] (complex 1), which clearly shows that the guest molecule SN is located at the port of Q[6]. The crystal structure analysis shows that the complex crystallized in the triclinic system and that the chiral space group was P21/n. The crystal data and parameters are shown in Table S1. Eight SNs are enriched around Q[6], of which four SNs are connected to the outer wall of Q[6] through hydrogen bonds, and the other four SNs act on the Q[6] port (Fig. 4 b). Fig. 4 c shows that the protonated SN molecules and Q[6] are driven by N1–H⋯O1, N1–H⋯O6, N2–H⋯C18, N2–H⋯N5, N2–H⋯C7 and C1–H⋯O2 hydrogen bonds to form a dense three-dimensional supramolecular framework without obvious holes (Fig. 4 d). At the same time, the thermal stability of complex 1 was tested by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). As shown in Fig. S8, the weight loss of complex 1 is about 13.7 % from room temperature to 175.6 °C, which is due to the loss of guest solvent molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of 2,4-DNP by the Q[6]-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence response of the Q[6]-CQDs to different phenolic compounds was investigated by fluorescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 5 a, when 2,4-DNP was added to the an aqueous solution of the Q[6]-CQDs, the fluorescence intensity at 350 nm was quenched, while the other phenol derivatives did not cause any significant changes in the fluorescence intensity of the Q[6]-CQDs. At the same time, we studied the effect of coexisting phenolic compounds on the recognition of 2,4-DNP by Q[6]-CQDs under the same conditions. Fig. 5 b and S9 proves that the presence of other phenolic compounds did not interfere with the detection of 2,4-DNP by the Q[6]-CQDs. Fig. 5 c shows the UV-Vis absorption spectra of Q[6]-CQDs with 2,4-DNP (0–30 μM), which shows that the intensity of the characteristic absorption peaks at 260 and 360 nm gradually increased.

The interaction mode between Q[6]-CQDs and 2,4-DNP was further investigated by 1H NMR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. S10, in the presence of Q[6]-CQDs, all proton peaks of 2,4-DNP shifted upfield and the chemical shift values of protons Ha, Hb and Hc shifted upfield by 0.12, 0.08, and 0.01 ppm. In addition, the 1H NMR spectra of the interaction between 2,4-DNP and Q[6] clearly show that the proton signal on the benzene ring of 2,4-DNP has a significant downfield shift (Fig. S11). This study shows that 2,4-DNP does not enter the cavity of Q[6]. In the SN and 2,4-DNP systems, it was observed that when the concentration of SN was increased, the proton signal of 2,4-DNP would shift upfield by different degrees (Fig. S12). According to the above results, 2,4-DNP may enter the shielding area of SN.

Combined with the crystal structure of complex 1, it is also proved that 2,4-DNP cannot be encapsulated in the cavity of cucurbit[6]uril and can only be adsorbed on the surface of the supramolecular framework formed by complex 1(Fig. 4 d, Fig. S13). It has been reported that if the distance between the luminescent sensor and the analyte is close, and the ultraviolet absorption spectrum of the analyte overlaps with the fluorescence emission spectrum of the sensor, a phenomenon called fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) occurs between the them [39]. As shown in Fig. 5 d, the fluorescence emission spectrum of Q[6]-CQDs has a large overlap with the UV-visible absorption spectrum of 2,4-DNP, which indicates that the fluorescence resonance energy transfer may be the reason why the fluorescence of Q[6]-CQDs solution is quenched by 2,4-DNP. In addition, the quenching mechanism was further confirmed by the Stern-Volmer equation [15]. As shown in Fig. S14, the emission spectra of Q[6]-CQDs with different 2,4-DNP concentrations were recorded. The F0/F curves obtained for 2,4-DNP concentrations in the range of 10–30 μM did not fit well to the Stern-Volmer equation. When the concentration of 2,4-DNP was in the range of 1.0 ∼ 10.0 μM, F0 / F had a good linear relationship with 2,4-DNP (R2 = 0.99), indicating that the quenching reaction started from dynamic quenching. With the increase of 2,4-DNP concentration, the bending curve gradually ascended up, describing the dynamic and static characteristics. In static quenching, the interaction between Q[6]-CQDs and 2,4-DNP led to the generation of non-fluorescent complexes. During the dynamic quenching process, the energy or charge transfer between the excited state of Q[6]-CQDs and 2,4-DNP was the primary reason for the fluorescence quenching.

The concentration of Q[6]-CQDs as a fluorescent probe was optimized to improve the sensitivity of the quantitative determination of 2,4-DNP. The fluorescence spectra obtained for Q[6]-CQDs solutions prepared at 10, 20, 40, 50, and 60 μg/mL before and after combining with 30 μM 2,4-DNP are shown in Fig. S15. When the concentration of Q[6]-CQDs was 40 μg/mL, the fluorescence quenching rate was the highest, with a value of 1.915, indicating that the Q[6]-CQDs solution at this concentration was the most sensitive in the fluorescence sensing of 2,4-DNP. Therefore, 40 μg/mL Q[6]-CQD solution was used for the subsequent quantitative determination of 2,4-DNP.

When the concentration of 2,4-DNP changes, the degree of fluorescence quenching of the Q[6]-CQDs is different. Fig. 6 a shows that with the continuous addition of 2,4-DNP, the fluorescence intensity of the Q[6]-CQDs observed at 351 nm gradually decreases until the concentration of 2,4-DNP reached 30 μM. Fig. 6 b shows that the decrease in the fluorescence of an aqueous solution of the Q[6]-CQDs exhibits a good linearity with the concentration of 2,4-DNP in the concentration range of 0–10 μM. The linear regression equation was I = −16.45C + 654.82; R2 = 0.9931, and the limit of detection was 0.15 μM . The standard curve was suitable for the determination of 2,4-DNP in the concentration range of 0–10 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative detection of 2,4-DNP in polluted water
ce:section.ce:para: Polluted water (3 mL) containing 2,4-DNP, which also contained common ions found in wastewater, as well as organic pollutants such as aniline, hydroquinone, and phenol, was used and a recovery experiment carried out according to the optimized determination conditions and repeated three times. The recovery rate was calculated, as shown in Table 2 , with the recovery in the range of 98.90–106.20 %. These results indicate that the use of Q[6]-CQDs as a fluorescent probe was suitable for the quantitative determination of 2,4-DNP in water.

In summary, we constructed fluorescent carbon quantum dots suitable for rapid detection of 2,4-DNP in aqueous solution. As a new type of fluorescent nanosensor, the Q[6]-CQDs can recognize and detect 2,4-DNP in an aqueous solution via sensitive changes in their fluorescence. The detection range was 0–10 μM, the standard curve was I = −16.45C + 654.82, R2 = 0.9931, and the detection limit was 0.15 μM, which was lower than the water quality criteria of 2,4-DNP for human health protection (1.63 μM) [40]. This quantitative method is suitable for the sensitive determination of 2,4-DNP in an aqueous solution, including the determination of 2,4-DNP in polluted water, within the recovery range of 98.90–106.20 %. Therefore, the construction of Q[6]-CQDs has found a new way for the application of cucurbit[n]urils, which feasibility and potential application in future fluorescence detection and recognition.

Xiaoqian Chen: Writing – original draft. Shangwei Yuan: Writing – review & editing. Yanan Ye: Writing – review & editing. Xinan Yang: Writing – review & editing. Xin Xiao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Peihua Ma: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 22161010).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2024.139731.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green synthesis of high photoluminescence nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots from grass via a simple hydrothermal method for removing organic and inorganic water pollutions
